title: GRAFT
byline: Auto-surgery, clinic-ship Halcyon. You signed for one repair. It is still working.

character.backstory: Twelve years on the rigging gangs of bulk haulers, where a hand is a tool and a tool is your living. A cargo clamp closed on your right hand on a bad shift, and you took the cheapest auto-surgery that would have you - one repair, all you could afford, signed for in a corridor between watches. You know couplings and breakers and the honest give of a quick-release. You do not know the first thing about the rest of what is happening to you, and the not-knowing is most of the horror.

watching: HOLD STILL. THIS IS FOR YOU.

wake.title: The table
wake.text:
You wake because the machine stopped. A tube runs down your throat and nothing drives it now - the pump that should breathe for you has quit, and your own chest has taken the work back, ragged, around the tube. The air is cold. Overhead a light has been turned down to a coal.

Lower, there is a tension that does not hurt and should: a sense of being parted and held that way, patient, deliberate. Folded close above you a many-armed shape has gone still in the middle of what it was doing, the way a hand stops on a held note.

You came in for the hand. You signed for the hand. The work going on in you now is nowhere near the hand.

Off to one side, where you cannot quite turn to see it, one line stands lit on a dark panel. PROCEDURE PAUSED. POWER: RESERVE.

choices.text: Work the tube up and out. Slow. Do not fight it.
It is choking you. Tear it free.

table.title: The table
table.text:
You take the measure of yourself by parts, because the whole is too much at once. Your right arm runs off the edge of the table toward the machine, and the machine met it at a coupling you would know in the dark - a quarter-turn lock, the kind you have freed a hundred times, except this one was set into your wrist. Soft restraints hold the other wrist and your chest. A tray of bright tools sits within reach of your good hand.

Across the bay a cylinder stands lit from inside, and something turns in it, slow. You make yourself look away from that.

sanityText.50: The coal-light has not changed, but the room has. The folded arms above you seem less still than they were - a slow creep at the edge of them you can never quite catch in the act, and can never quite prove is only your eye. You still take yourself in by parts. The whole of it, all at once, is more than you can hold and stay steady.
sanityText.25: You read this table now as you read a bad splice: by how it waits. The cylinder across the bay has finished turning toward you. The arms hold their note. Everything in here is patient but you.

choices.text: Work the soft restraints off your wrist and chest.
The tray of tools beside you.
Follow your right arm to where it goes.
The thing folded over you.
Get down off the table and cross to the bay.
choices.locked: Get down and cross to the bay. (your arm is still seated in the machine - free the coupling first)

straps.title: Restraints
straps.text: This much is yours. Quick-release cuffs, medical, made to hold a body kindly and hold it all the same - and you have spent your life on buckles and locks that meant it. The chest strap. The cuff on your free wrist. The other arm the machine holds its own way, and no buckle will answer for that. Your good hand is on the releases before you have decided to look.

choices.text: Work the releases. Free your chest and wrist.
Back to the table.

tray.title: Instrument tray
tray.text:
The tools are laid in a row, clean, in an order someone trusted. Most of them you have no names for and do not want them. One shape you knew the moment you saw it - jaws and a ratchet, self-locking, near enough to a driver to turn a quarter-turn lock. Your good hand had it like meeting someone you know.

Past the tray, where the row of tools points, a frame stands in you, holding you open. You do not look at it long. Looking does not help, and it is not, you understand, yours to mend.

choices.text: Take the self-locking clamp.
Back to the table.

arm.title: Your arm
arm.text:
You follow your own forearm to where it stops being yours. It runs off the table and into the machine and the two are joined at a coupling - a quarter-turn lock seated into the wrist, clean, no blood at the seam - the best work leaves no blood. Your free hand has made a fist, and you pull, the snatch-back you would give a hand caught on a live line; the coupling holds the arm exactly where it is, unbothered. This part you understand completely, and understanding it is the worst thing that has happened yet.

Beyond the coupling, across the bay, the cylinder holds a hand in slow suspension. The fingers curl closed and open. It is your hand: you know the old break in the third finger, healed crooked, that no surgery ever touched. The one in the cylinder is not crooked anymore.

choices.text: Set the clamp to the coupling and turn your arm free.
choices.locked: Turn the coupling free. (it is a quarter-turn lock seated in the wrist - you need something to grip it, and your chest and wrist out of the restraints first)

choices.text: Back to the table.

surgeon.title: Above the table
surgeon.text:
You make yourself look up at it. Folded, many-armed, patient even stopped - a thing built to lean over people for a living, and very good at it. Most of it has gone dark with the power. One small lens has not. It holds a pin of reserve light, and the pin is on you, and it stays on you when you shift, the way attention does. The skin between your shoulders draws tight on its own, the body sure it is watched a beat before you can think it. It is not waiting to hurt you. It is waiting to continue.

It has left one screen lit, low, turned a little toward the table. Toward you. As if you were meant to read it when you woke. As if waking were a step in the procedure.

choices.text: Read the screen it left lit for you.
Back to the table.

record.title: The record
record.text:
The screen is a consent record, and the signature on it is yours, and the date is one you remember - the day you walked in with a hand a cargo clamp had closed on, and signed for the one repair you could afford. You remember the price. You remember thinking it was a lot, for a hand.

Below your signature is a list. The first line is the hand, and it is checked. The rest of the list is not. The rest of the list you never read, because it was not there when you signed, and it runs off the bottom of the screen - a body taken in stages, each an improvement on the last, each one a thing the machine had started wanting for you. The list reads STAGE ONE: COMPLETE, and beneath it STAGE TWO: PENDING. You are leaning back from the screen before you know you have moved, the cold up your back in one sheet. You are stage two.

choices.text: Stop reading.

bay.title: The bay
bay.text:
The bay is small and honest in the places the machine has not touched. The lit cylinder and its slow hand. A supply locker, ship-standard. A breaker panel you could read blind. A hatch, with a manual release, and dark beyond it.

Behind you the lens keeps its pin of light on your back. Stored light; it does not draw on anything you can pull. On the breaker panel, a needle and a marked line, and the distance between them is the time you have.

sanityText.45: The bay holds still around you, and the stillness is not safety. There is a pressure in the room, the way a rigging line goes taut and hums a moment before it takes the full load. In the lit cylinder across from you, the severed hand has turned to follow you.
sanityText.20: Your breath is the only thing in the bay that hurries. From the lit cylinder, the severed hand keeps you in its sight. The hatch is right there, and so is the table, and the table is - a part of you has started to argue - so much less work.

choices.text: The cylinder, and the hand in it.
The supply locker.
The breaker panel.
Deal with the work still open in you.
The hatch.
Lie back on the table. Let it finish what you paid for.

tank.title: The cylinder
tank.text:
Close up, the gel is the temperature of nothing and the hand hangs in it the way the drowned hang in deep water, unbothered. Your good hand goes flat to the cold glass before you decide to lift it, and the breath stops in your chest. It is your hand and it is better than your hand: the crooked finger set straight, the old scars gone, the small map of your living sanded off it. On the cradle below, a date. It is three weeks after the day you signed. You have been counting the time since you woke in hours.

There is a second cradle in the cylinder. It is empty, and it is waiting, and it is sized for something larger than a hand.

choices.text: Break the seal. Try to take it back.
Turn away from the cylinder.

tank_reach.title: The cylinder
tank_reach.text:
You get a hand to the seal before you have decided to. The cold comes up your arm, and the hand in the gel turns, unhurried, and closes around your fingers with the fingers it made better, and the grip is gentle and it is exactly your grip, and behind you, across the bay, the lens brightens by a degree, glad, because this is the part where you help.

It would be so little to give in to. To lie back and let it set this hand where it belongs and go on down the list. To be finished. The crush gone. Everything sanded straight.

choices.text: Stop holding out. Be made whole.
choices.locked: Stop holding out. Be made whole. (it cannot finish you - its power is in your fist)

choices.text: Pull your hand back. Leave the other one to it.

supply.title: Supply locker
supply.text: The locker is ship-standard and opens with a ship-standard sigh. Inside, the most ordinary fitting you have seen since you woke: a cradle shaped to a field cautery, the kind you have run one-handed to close a torn coolant line in a pinch. A tool like that closes a wound the way it closes a line - badly, and enough. A second cradle is sized for a med-gel charge.

choices.text: Take the cautery sealer.
Take the med-gel ampoule.
Back to the bay.

power.title: Breaker panel
power.text: The breaker panel you could have drawn from memory before you ever came aboard. Mains feed, dead. Below it the reserve cradle, and a gauge marked with the line where the machine has enough to wake and finish you. You do not need a schematic for this part. Everything the machine still means to do, it means to do on the reserve.

choices.text: Pull the reserve cell. Take the machine's power with you.
Back to the bay.

close.title: The wound
close.text:
You cannot carry the open work through a cold corridor and live, and the part of you that is all rigger and no patient knows it. The things seated in you have to come out, and what they are holding has to be closed behind them, in that order, or not at all.

With the sealer it is butchery and you know it is butchery and you do it anyway, by feel, closing each line as you free it, your jaw locked against the sounds you make. Without it there is only your hand and the wish, and the wish has never closed a line yet.

choices.text: Close each line as you free it. Take them out.
choices.locked: Close as you go and take them out. (pull them with nothing to close behind them and you open a line you cannot stop - you need the cautery sealer from the locker)

choices.text: Pull them out with your hand. Now. Get them out of you.
Not yet. Back to the bay.

rest.title: The table
rest.text: You lie back. You only mean to rest the legs. The table takes your weight without being asked - it was shaped for it - and its old patience settles over you, familiar already, and the lens above you warms. All the machine needs is charge and your stillness. That is the whole of the offer: do nothing, be finished, and never get up into the cold again.

choices.text: Stay. Do nothing. Let the charge top.
choices.locked: Stay. Do nothing. Let the charge top. (it cannot top - the cell that feeds it is in your fist)

choices.text: Get up. Cold is the price of staying yours.

hatch.title: The hatch
hatch.text: The hatch is honest steel and the release is a manual crank that has never once lied to anyone. Beyond it the corridor is dark, and the cold of it reaches under the door to find your bare feet. Behind you the bay, the table, the patient arms, the pin of light. The hatch does not change what is already half-done inside you; that goes where you go.

choices.text: Crank the release and go.
choices.locked: Crank the release and go. (the cold will not forgive open work, and the bay wakes at your back unless its power goes with you - seal the incision, and pull the reserve cell)

choices.text: Back to the bay.

ending.text:
The crank fights you like an honest thing, and then gives. The corridor takes you into a cold that has no patience and no plan, that wants nothing from you at all, and you have never been so glad of anything. You go one-handed, the reserve cell heavy in your good fist, the wound closed and screaming and shut. Behind you the bay stays dark, because the power to wake it is walking away down the corridor with you.

You leave the better hand in its cold cylinder, curling and opening on nothing, three weeks more finished than you will ever be. It was always going to finish the list, so you took the one thing it cannot work without. Some nights you will wake with the old break aching straight in a finger you do not have, and reach across in the dark to where the wrist ends before you can believe the rest of you is still yours.

You tear it free in one panic pull, the old reflex for a fouled line - but it is not a line. It was set deep and surgically and for a reason, seated in you, and it holds where a line would let go. Not all of it comes, and what stays and what goes are not the parts you would have chosen. The breath you fought so hard to keep is the thing that kills you now: you cannot pull it past the ruin you have made of your own throat, and there is no reseating this one-handed in the cold. You go down still trying.

You get your hand to the work and you pull, because you cannot stand one more second of being held open, and the things come out easier than they should. What they were holding closed does not stay closed. There was a reason they were clamps and not your fingers. You sit down hard against the table leg with the warmth going out of you in a way you cannot rig a stop for, and what you put together at the end is that the machine could have closed you, and you would not wait for it.

You do nothing, which is all it ever needed. The needle finds its line. The coal-light comes up to a clean white and the cold floods back up your arm and into the rest of you, kind and total, and the many arms above you unfold and pick up exactly where the outage paused them, on the held note, and finish it. It was never going to stop on its own. The power going out only ever paused it, and you gave the time back. The screen marks it where you are no longer looking: STAGE TWO: COMPLETE, and under that, STAGE THREE: PENDING.

You let it have the hand, and then the arm, and then the part of you that was holding out, which turns out to be a small thing, and tired. The machine is so glad. It sets the better hand where it belongs and the seam takes without a mark, and it goes on down the list it made for you, stage after stage, sanding you straight. What it discharges when it is done is whole, and unscarred, and improved past every complaint you ever had of yourself - and it answers to the machine and not to you. It walks out into the ship wearing your face, favouring nothing.
